1.synthesis: Chapter 36 is one of the most historically significant passages in the book of Jeremiah for what it reveals about the formation of prophetic literature. In the fourth year of Jehoiakim — 605/604 BC, the year of the Battle of Carchemish and the beginning of Babylonian world dominance — YHWH commands Jeremiah to produce a written scroll containing all the prophetic words spoken since the beginning of his ministry in Josiah’s day (627 BC). The purpose is explicitly evangelistic: “perhaps the house of Judah will hear all the disaster I plan to bring upon them, so that everyone will turn from his evil way” (v.3). Jeremiah dictates to Baruch son of Neriah, a trained scribe who writes the words in ink on a scroll. Then Baruch is commissioned to read the scroll publicly on a fast day when Jerusalem will be full of people from the surrounding towns. The passage introduces Baruch as more than a secretary; he is a trusted prophetic collaborator whose social standing and scribal competence give the prophetic word access to public and official space that Jeremiah himself has lost.
1.historical_context: The fourth year of Jehoiakim (605/604 BC) was one of the most consequential years in ANE history. Nebuchadnezzar defeated the Egyptians at Carchemish, ending Egyptian dominance of the Levant. Soon after, Daniel and his companions were taken to Babylon (Dan. 1:1–3). Jeremiah had been preaching since 627 BC — over twenty years of accumulated oracles now reduced to a single scroll. Baruch son of Neriah son of Mahseiah was from a prominent family: his brother Seraiah was a senior court official (Jer. 51:59). A clay bulla (seal impression) inscribed “belonging to Berekhyahu [Baruch] son of Neriyahu [Neriah] the scribe” was discovered in the antiquities market in the 1970s, providing remarkable archaeological corroboration of Baruch’s existence and scribal status. Jeremiah’s restriction from the Temple (v.5) may reflect an official ban following his Temple Sermon of chapter 7.
1.literary_structure: The unit (vv.1–8) follows the commission-execution pattern: divine command (vv.1–3), Jeremiah’s compliance via dictation to Baruch (v.4), instructions to Baruch (vv.5–7), and Baruch’s execution (v.8). The repeated “perhaps” (ʾulay) in v.3 and v.7 is theologically significant: it does not signal divine uncertainty but the genuine open nature of human response. YHWH does not override human freedom in the process of prophetic proclamation. The commissioning of Baruch introduces a proxy-reading structure that will be sustained throughout the chapter, as the word moves through multiple audiences before reaching its destined audience — the king.
1.theological_application: The production of the written scroll represents a significant theological development: prophetic proclamation is committed to writing to outlast the prophet’s immediate access to audiences. Writing enables the word to go where Jeremiah cannot, to persist beyond a single reading, to be reproduced when destroyed. The explicit evangelistic purpose of the scroll — “perhaps they will turn” — reflects the NT parallel in John 20:31: “these are written so that you may believe.” Scripture itself is a form of grace — the accommodation of the divine word to human capacity for deferral and repetition. The partnership between Jeremiah’s prophetic speech and Baruch’s scribal service also models the collaborative nature of canonical formation: the Spirit-inspired prophet and the Spirit-guided community together produce and preserve the word.
1.christology: Jeremiah’s scroll, dictated in response to divine commission for the salvation of those who will hear and turn, foreshadows the formation of the New Testament itself — words spoken by the Word made flesh, transmitted through apostolic witnesses, written down so that future generations might hear and believe. The evangelistic purpose of writing (“perhaps they will turn”) finds its NT counterpart in the explicit aim of the Gospel of John (John 20:31) and the Apocalypse (Rev. 1:3). Baruch, as the faithful scribe who channels the prophet’s words into written form, functions as a type of the apostolic and scribal tradition through which the words of Jesus were preserved. Christ himself is the living scroll — the Word of God in person — whose words are committed to writing that others might have life through them (John 5:39–40).

9.synthesis: In December 604 BC — shortly after the fall of Ashkelon to Nebuchadnezzar and likely during a fast called in response to the Babylonian advance — Baruch reads the scroll from a prominent chamber in the Temple precinct. The chain of transmission that follows is carefully documented: the scroll is read to the people in the Temple, then reported by Micaiah son of Gemariah to the senior court officials gathered in the king’s house, then read a second time directly to the officials by Baruch himself. The officials’ response — they “were afraid at one another” (v.16) and declared that the king must be told — is the first genuinely alarmed reaction to Jeremiah’s word in the chapter. They take the scroll seriously enough to both warn Baruch and Jeremiah to hide and to report its contents to the palace. The chain-of-transmission structure serves a narrative purpose: it dramatises how the prophetic word travels through the social hierarchy toward the king who will ultimately reject it.
9.historical_context: The date — “the ninth month of the fifth year of Jehoiakim” (December 604 BC) — coincides closely with the period after Ashkelon’s destruction by Nebuchadnezzar, which would have prompted widespread fasting throughout Judah as the Babylonian threat became concrete. Baruch reads from the chamber of Gemariah son of Shaphan — the Shaphan family were consistent supporters of Jeremiah (cf. Jer. 26:24; 29:3). The officials named in v.12 — Elishama the scribe, Delaiah son of Shemaiah, Elnathan son of Achbor, Gemariah son of Shaphan, Zedekiah son of Hananiah — represent the senior royal administration. The officials’ alarm (v.16) and their warning to Baruch to hide suggests that at least some members of the court took seriously the danger that the scroll’s contents posed, both theologically and politically.
9.literary_structure: The elaborate chain-of-transmission narrative (vv.9–19) functions as a sustained build-up of dramatic tension toward the king’s reception of the scroll in vv.20–26. Each link in the chain — people to Micaiah to officials to Baruch — adds a layer of authority and social weight to the scroll. The officials’ procedural interrogation (“how did you write all these words? Was it from his mouth?” v.17) seeks to establish the legal provenance of the document: was it oral dictation (and thus authentically Jeremianic) or literary composition (and thus potentially Baruch’s creative work)? Baruch’s answer confirms the scribal relationship precisely. The officials’ warning to hide signals that the coming royal encounter will be dangerous — building reader anticipation for the scene to follow.
9.theological_application: The public reading of Scripture and its transmission through community witnesses is a pattern established here that has shaped Jewish and Christian practice ever since. The Levitical tradition of public Torah reading (Deut. 31:10–13), the synagogue reading tradition, and the NT instruction for public reading of the apostolic letters (Col. 4:16; 1 Tim. 4:13) all reflect this principle: the written word of God is meant to be read aloud in community, processed through communal response, and allowed to move through the social body. The officials who hear the scroll and are alarmed illustrate that God’s word is capable of producing genuine conviction even in secular power structures — though, as the following scene shows, conviction without surrender produces defensive rejection rather than repentance.
9.christology: The chain of testimony by which the scroll reaches its intended audience prefigures the NT pattern of gospel transmission: from Jesus to the apostles to their witnesses to successive generations. The interrogation “how did you write this? Was it from his mouth?” echoes questions asked of the disciples about their testimony (“how do you know these things?”) and the Pharisees’ interrogation of the man born blind (John 9:26): “what did he do to you?” In both cases, the authoritative chain of origin is the decisive question. Baruch’s answer — “he dictated all these words to me and I wrote them with ink on the scroll” — parallels the apostolic claim that they wrote what they received (1 Cor. 15:3): the authority of the written word derives from the spoken word of the one who commissioned it.

20.synthesis: The scene in the winter palace with Jehoiakim burning the scroll column by column as Jehudi reads it is one of the most dramatically charged episodes in all of prophetic literature. The king’s response is the inverse of his father Josiah’s reaction to the discovered law book (2 Kgs. 22:11–13): Josiah tore his robes and wept; Jehoiakim tears the scroll and burns it. The court around him was not unanimous — three officials urged him not to burn it (v.25) — but neither the king nor the remaining attendants showed fear. The burning is not impulsive destruction but deliberate, methodical rejection: as Jehudi reads three or four columns, the king cuts them off and feeds them to the fire, a calculated act of counter-authority claiming that the royal will can annul the prophetic word. The chapter’s implicit theology is clear: it cannot. The attempt to suppress the word of God by physical destruction always fails because the word is not contained in the material medium through which it travels.
20.historical_context: The winter house (v.22) with its brazier burning in the ninth month (Kislev, November-December) reflects the seasonal alternation between summer and winter palaces documented in other royal residences of the period. The scribe’s knife (taʿar hasopher, v.23) was a standard scribal tool for cutting and trimming papyrus or leather scrolls — Jehoiakim weaponises a scribal instrument against the scribal product. The officials who urged the king not to burn the scroll — Elnathan, Delaiah, and Gemariah (v.25) — include the Gemariah in whose chamber Baruch had first read the scroll. The contrast with Josiah’s response to the law book is a deliberate Deuteronomistic counterpoint: the same medium (a scroll, a public reading, a royal response) produces diametrically opposite outcomes in father and son, tracing the covenantal arc of the Davidic dynasty’s collapse.
20.literary_structure: The scene is choreographed with care: the scroll deposited in Elishama’s chamber (v.20), the king summoned while sitting by the fire (vv.21–22), the reading of columns followed by their immediate destruction (v.23), the dissenting voices silenced (vv.24–25), and the futile arrest order (v.26). The note that “YHWH had hidden them” (v.26) provides the chapter’s quiet theological punchline: the king’s authority does not reach where YHWH hides his servants. The scene’s dramatic irony is complete — the reader knows what the king does not: the scroll he burns will be reconstituted with additions, and the king who burned it will be judged by its words.
20.theological_application: The burning of the scroll is a textbook instance of the futility of suppressing God’s word through power. The history of Christian witness through persecution, censorship, and destruction of Bibles consistently demonstrates the same pattern: burning books does not extinguish the truth they contain. The Reformation’s survival despite papal condemnation, the Chinese church’s growth through decades of anti-religious suppression, and the underground persistence of Scripture throughout church history all bear witness to the principle enacted here. Jehoiakim’s methodical destruction — cutting column by column and burning section by section — models the deliberate and rationalised rejection of God’s word that characterises unbelief at its most self-conscious: not ignorance but wilful suppression of known truth (Rom. 1:18).
20.christology: Jehoiakim’s burning of the scroll is a concentrated image of the world’s rejection of the Word, which finds its ultimate expression in the crucifixion: the logos burned alive on the cross of human hatred. John’s Gospel opens with “the Word was made flesh” (John 1:14) and the world “did not receive him” (John 1:11). Just as the scroll was not destroyed but reconstituted — with additions that included judgment on the destroyer — so Christ was not destroyed by the cross but raised with a body bearing the wounds of human rejection, wounds that become the basis of his eternal intercession. The king who killed the word found that he had not silenced it but confirmed it.

27.synthesis: Following the burning of the scroll, YHWH commands the production of a second scroll containing all the former words, plus an additional body of judgment oracles specifically targeting Jehoiakim and his dynasty. The reconstitution of the scroll demonstrates that the prophetic word is not dependent on any physical medium: it exists in the mind and mouth of God and can be re-instantiated at will. The oracle against Jehoiakim is pointedly personal: he will have no descendant to sit on David’s throne; his corpse will be exposed to heat and frost without burial (v.30). The judgment against his servants and offspring rounds out the indictment. The closing note that the second scroll contained “many similar words added to them” (v.32) is the chapter’s quiet theological triumph: Jehoiakim’s attempt to diminish the prophetic word through destruction actually expanded it. His rejection of the word generated more word. The chapter closes on an irony that has echoed through the entire history of biblical persecution: silencing the prophets does not reduce the prophetic corpus; it enlarges it.
27.historical_context: The oracle that Jehoiakim will have “no one to sit on the throne of David” (v.30) is fulfilled ironically: his son Jehoiachin did succeed him but reigned only three months before being deported to Babylon (2 Kgs. 24:8–12). The judgment that his corpse would be exposed to heat and frost (v.30) implies an ignoble, unburied death, contrasting with the royal burial Jehoiakim might have expected. Jeremiah 22:18–19 amplifies this: “he shall be buried with the burial of a donkey, dragged and dumped beyond the gates of Jerusalem.” The “many similar words added to them” (v.32) is historically significant for the study of prophetic redaction: it indicates that the Jeremiah scroll was understood as expansible, capable of receiving additional material as circumstances warranted, and that Baruch was the agent of this expansion.
27.literary_structure: The section (vv.27–32) follows a clean structure: the divine command to reconstitute the scroll (vv.27–28), the oracle against Jehoiakim (vv.29–31), and the execution of the reconstitution (v.32). The oracle against Jehoiakim is delivered in second person (“you burned this scroll saying...”) before shifting to third person judgment, creating a moment of direct divine accusation. The oracle specifically references the king’s stated rationale for burning the scroll — his objection to the Babylonian invasion prediction — rebutting it directly: the very king of Babylon against whom he burned the warning will be the instrument of his dynasty’s end. The chapter ends with Baruch writing a second scroll, an act of quiet faithfulness that preserves the word for all subsequent generations.
27.theological_application: The reconstituted scroll — longer and more damning than the one destroyed — embodies the theological principle that God’s word is indestructible (Isa. 40:8: “the word of our God will stand forever”). Every attempt to suppress prophetic truth in the history of the church has produced the same pattern: persecution pruned back the church externally while producing internal depth and textual richness. The specific oracle against Jehoiakim for burning the scroll underscores that YHWH takes seriously attacks on the vehicles of his communication: the destruction of Scripture is not a neutral bibliographic act but a covenantal assault with consequences proportionate to the dignity of what was attacked. Jehoiakim destroyed a scroll; he was judged by its contents. The act of wilful suppression becomes the ground of personal indictment.
27.christology: The reconstituted scroll with additions parallels the NT canon that emerges from the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus: the Word that the powers of the world attempted to silence becomes, through that very rejection, more abundant and more widely distributed. The NT is itself an “expanded scroll” that includes, among its contents, the explicit record of human rejection of the Word and the divine judgment that rejection incurs. Baruch’s faithful re-writing despite the danger he faced typifies the apostolic and scribal tradition that preserved and transmitted the words of Jesus at personal cost. The canon we hold is, in this sense, a reconstituted scroll — gathered, curated, and preserved by those who carried Jeremiah’s mantle into the new covenant era.
